text: The surgeon identifies the midpoint between the twelfth rib and the iliac border along the mid-axillary line as the optimal location to establish the retroperitoneal space for the procedure.

After establishing the retroperitoneal space, the surgeon places two additional trocars for robotic arms one and two. The first trocar is positioned at the same level as the camera, maintaining a distance of at least eight centimeters from it.

The surgeon positions the second arm trocar along the posterior axillary line, ensuring it is eight centimeters away from the camera line. The system trocar is placed two centimeters anterior to the iliac border to avoid the colon, followed by enlarging the posterior space.
The surgeon removes fat tissue from the retroperitoneal space to enhance the visibility of anatomical structures.
The surgeon gradually enlarges the retroperitoneal space by pushing away the surrounding fat tissue, visibly increasing the available surgical area.
The surgeon cleans the space outside Gerota's fascia by removing the surrounding fat tissue to reveal clear anatomical landmarks.
The surgeon identifies the anatomical landmark of the upper genophageal region, noting the retroperitoneal reflection. The posterior survey reveals the closed source muscle in the surgical field.
The surgeon continues to expand the surgical space, opening the surrounding tissue until reaching the surface of the kidney, revealing its anatomical features.
The surgeon identifies the plane outside the kidney surface, preparing to approach the vessels after clearing the surrounding fat tissue.
The surgeon isolates the anterior surface near the junction, revealing the vessel. The vessel is visible, highlighting the challenge of approaching it before reaching the junction.
The surgeon rotates and pushes the kidney to isolate it, aiming to obtain a better view of the tumor's location for assessment.
The surgeon isolates the renal sinus space, revealing the tumor's location. The camera captures the visualization of the venous structures within the renal sinus.
An instrument is shown with a needle emerging from its tip, likely used for precise surgical tasks.
